paragraphs.body: I remember walking into a gym and seeing two courts next to one another. The strong smell of newly waxed floors came rushing to me. I was so anxious, having never played before, with the majority of girls having played. We all removed our outdoor shoes and put on court shoes and kneepads to play. Mine were new, bright white. Everyone’s kneepads but mine had holes and were a light shade of brown from being so worn down. The coach yelled, "Circle up"! We all hustled to get there not wanting to be last. All 80 girls stood in a circle with the coaches at one end. The coaches already knew who I was but had yet to meet them. Then they hey split us into groups based on skill. I only knew a few girls from my elementary school who happened to be in my group. Within those groups, we were given different drills based on skill. Having never played, I was in the low-skill group, so I had to partner to do simple drills like toss and catch. That simple drill got me hooked, and I have never looked back.

annotations.text: The strong smell of newly waxed floors came rushing to me.
annotations.comment: I love this smell! Great sensory detail :)

paragraphs.body: Volleyball started as an activity my mom used to get me out of the house. I was hesitant, never enjoying any sports I played before. I went in with the assumption I was not going to like it, but to my surprise, I came up with the opposite opinion. Volleyball soon became a place I could look forward to. Those two hours of practice, twice a week, became a highlight of my week.

There was nothing complex about volleyball. There was no pressure, which created a safe environment where I could truly grow. When I first started, my coaches cared not about winning but strictly about player growth, wanting to see players become their best. There was no punishment for making mistakes because coaches wanted you to learn. Everyone played in the games; there were no starters because playing time was how you got better.

annotations.text: There was nothing complex about volleyball. There was no pressure, which created a safe environment where I could truly grow.

paragraphs.body: This view of my sport changed quickly. Just over a year after I started playing, I decided to take an offer on a more competitive team. I soon learned this team only cared about winning. It was a toxic environment, putting girls against one another for a starting spot. This was the start of hurtful coaches in my seven years of playing.

“One moment, pure love, a fiery thrill,” “The next, a fall, a shattered will.” “But in time, that spark reignites,” “And the love returns, burning bright.” My high school years were some of the worst years I faced while playing. I joined the program in eighth grade, two years after I started playing. I did not know much before joining; I had just moved and was not in high school yet. I made the team and played on JV for my eighth and freshman years. I played all summer before sophomore year, hoping to make varsity. Tryouts rolled around, and four spots were open; he took three girls and left me on JV. When he told me, he said, "We don't have room for you on the team," and "You will practice with varsity sometimes because we need you for next year." I later found out he took a girl who did not go to tryouts because she was sick. My coach already knew who he wanted, but it led me to believe I stood a chance. I practiced with varsity once in a while that year, each time being extremely anxious because I was not on the team. "What if the girls didn't want me there?" or if "I wasn't good enough" to be playing is what started to play in my head. I was on the team starting the following year but later realized what that came with. We were not as good as we had been in the past that year, having a tied record by the end of the season. My coach blamed a singular player for the loss, sometimes even if we won. He would say, "You need to be doing better," or "What is going on?"

annotations.text: "What if the girls didn't want me there?" or if "I wasn't good enough" to be playing is what started to play in my head.
annotations.comment: great details! This voice and feeling is authentic and gives a lot of credibility to the essay.
annotations.context_right: I was on the team s

annotations.comment: This poem creates an interesting moment of foreshadowing. It seems to predict the future a bit :)
annotations.context_left: “The next, a fall, a shattered will.”

paragraphs.body: By my senior year, I was used to what he had to say to players. I thought I knew everything that could happen during this season, but I was completely wrong. That year, a junior transfer played my position and ended up beating me out. My coach split the team in half, with teammates taking sides for me or the other girl. When it was announced, he did it in front of the whole team, just saying the starting lineup, "Sam as defensive specialist and Olivia as libero." He never said anything to me until I had to approach him because I was not playing well with her. After all, we were both controlling on the court due to our positions. When I briefly mentioned at the end of our conversation how he announced the positions hurt me, he said he "didn't even realize." I had played under him for five years at the time, and he made me feel like a terrible player and not respected as a person.

annotations.text: I had played under him for five years at the time, and he made me feel like a terrible player and not respected as a person.

paragraphs.body: When I think of volleyball now, I do not think of the bad coaches. Instead, I think of how it grew me as a person. Those bad coaches who made me start to hate the sport I once loved made me a stronger person. Never having been a starter until later in my career, I am a hard worker. Being told I was not good enough I gained resilience. Behind all the bad, there were some good coaches who gave me confidence as a player and a person.

annotations.text: When I think of volleyball now, I do not think of the bad coaches. Instead, I think of how it grew me as a person. Those bad coaches who made me start to hate the sport I once loved made me a stronger person. Never having been a starter until later in my career, I am a hard worker. Being told I was not good enough I gained resilience. Behind all the bad, there were some good coaches who gave me confidence as a player and a person.

paragraphs.body: I have not made these negative experiences positive on my own. The public eye has criticized Taylor Swift for years. In 2017, Kayne West wrote a song lyric that said, “I feel like me and Taylor might have sex.” Swift came out saying she did not approve of this lyric. However, West posted a fake phone call online, with Swift approving the lyrics. This led the internet to call Swift a liar, leading her into hiding for over a year. Since then, she released six albums, was named TIME Person of the Year, and passed her own record for producing the highest-grossing tour of all time with her current tour “The Eras Tour”. Swift turned an experience that could’ve ended her music career into a record-breaking albums and tours. She has shown me that no matter what happens, nobody can break your passion for something you genuinely love. Swift has shown me that no person should make me want to quit my passion. She has shown me how to come out of a situation better due to a person that makes you feel like less.

annotations.text: I have not made these negative experiences positive on my own.
annotations.comment: I'm not quite sure what this sentence means, here, or what the connection to volleyball is? Perhaps you could write a few more lines to explain and connect. :)

annotations.text: She has shown me that no matter what happens, nobody can break your passion for something you genuinely love.

paragraphs.body: I did those toss-and-pass drills until my last practice. They evolved a little over the years, with me passing on my side, having to shuffle to the ball, and even passing back and forth occasionally. Those drills were simple at heart, and easy at the end of the day. You did not have to think about what you were doing; it was just volleyball.

annotations.text: Those drills were simple at heart, and easy at the end of the day. You did not have to think about what you were doing; it was just volleyball.
